intro: Artemis II is finally off the pad — humans are heading to the Moon for the first time in over 50 years — while the AI world is consumed by the Claude Code source leak and OpenAI's staggering $122B funding round. A lot happened.

stories.title: Artemis II launches four astronauts toward the Moon
stories.summary: At 6:35 PM EDT on Wednesday, NASA's Space Launch System lifted off from Cape Canaveral carrying four astronauts on the Artemis II mission — the first crewed flight to the vicinity of the Moon since Apollo 17 in 1972. The mission is a cislunar free-return trajectory, meaning the crew will loop around the Moon and return to Earth without landing, serving as a critical shakedown of the Orion capsule's life support and navigation systems with humans on board. The four-person crew includes NASA astronauts Reid Wiseman, Victor Glover, and Christina Koch, along with Canadian Space Agency astronaut Jeremy Hansen. This flight follows years of delays and cost overruns for the SLS program, which has been a perennial lightning rod for criticism compared to SpaceX's cheaper Starship approach. Still, the mission represents a genuine milestone: no human has left low Earth orbit since December 1972, and this crew will travel farther from Earth than any human in history. The Orion capsule was previously validated on the uncrewed Artemis I flight in late 2022, which completed a 25-day mission. Artemis II is expected to last about 10 days. Hacker News gave the NASA live blog a score of 782, making it the single most-engaged story of the day by a wide margin — a reminder that space still captures imaginations even in a crowded news cycle. If all goes well, Artemis III — which would actually land on the lunar South Pole — could follow within the next 18 months, using SpaceX's Human Landing System variant of Starship. For now, this is the real deal: people are going to the Moon.

stories.title: SpaceX files confidentially for IPO, targeting a $1.75 trillion valuation
stories.summary: SpaceX has submitted a confidential S-1 filing to the SEC, kicking off what would be the largest IPO in history if it proceeds at its target valuation of $1.75 trillion. For context, that would make SpaceX more valuable than all but a handful of the world's largest companies — roughly on par with Alphabet and well above Meta. The filing was long anticipated; SpaceX has been running secondary share sales for years to provide liquidity to employees and early investors, but a full public listing changes the game significantly in terms of disclosure requirements and governance. Elon Musk has previously been coy about timing, but the confluence of Artemis II launching (highlighting SpaceX's centrality to the US space program), strong Starlink subscriber growth, and a generally hot IPO market seems to have accelerated the decision. The BBC notes this could make Musk the world's first trillionaire, given his equity stake. Hacker News users engaged heavily with the NYT's coverage, scoring it 275. The valuation is ambitious — SpaceX generated roughly $15 billion in revenue in 2025 according to estimates, meaning the target multiple is eye-watering even by tech standards. Starlink's recurring subscription revenue is likely the anchor for that valuation, since launch services alone wouldn't justify it. Investors will want to scrutinize how SpaceX accounts for Starship development costs, which are enormous. The confidential filing means public details are still sparse, but expect a prospectus within months.

stories.title: OpenAI raises $122 billion in new funding to expand frontier AI globally
stories.summary: OpenAI has closed a $122 billion funding round — yes, that number is not a typo — to fund frontier AI research, next-generation compute infrastructure, and to meet surging demand for ChatGPT, Codex, and enterprise AI products. This is a staggering sum that dwarfs even the largest previous tech funding rounds and reflects both the scale of capital OpenAI believes it needs to compete and the appetite investors have for AI exposure. The round is part of a broader trend: Q1 2026 startup funding shattered all records, largely driven by mega-deals into OpenAI, Anthropic, xAI, and Waymo. OpenAI has been on a spending spree on compute, most visibly through the Stargate joint venture with SoftBank and Oracle, which is building out massive US-based data center capacity. The new capital will also support OpenAI's ongoing restructuring from a capped-profit entity toward a more conventional for-profit structure — a process that has faced legal challenges from Elon Musk. For enterprise buyers, this signals OpenAI isn't going anywhere and can make long-term infrastructure commitments. For competitors like Anthropic and Google DeepMind, it raises the competitive bar significantly. The round also comes as OpenAI has been aggressively expanding its model lineup, with GPT-4.1, GPT-5.4 mini, and nano variants all apparently now in production use based on Gradient Labs' announcement. Whether this level of capital deployment will produce proportional capability gains — given that scaling returns appear to be flattening — is the central open question in AI right now.

stories.title: Oracle hits thousands of employees with mass layoffs
stories.summary: Oracle has conducted a significant round of layoffs, with reports suggesting thousands of employees have lost their jobs — though the company has not disclosed an official figure. This is notable because Oracle has been one of the primary beneficiaries of the AI infrastructure boom: the company is a core partner in OpenAI's Stargate data center buildout and has been reporting strong cloud revenue growth driven by AI workloads. The layoffs appear to be concentrated in legacy business units as Oracle aggressively pivots its workforce toward AI and cloud-native roles, a pattern seen across the industry. Oracle CEO Safra Catz has been explicit in recent quarters about restructuring headcount toward higher-leverage AI-adjacent roles. The timing is somewhat ironic given Oracle's prominent position in the AI investment narrative. For affected workers, the pattern is familiar: companies using AI revenue growth as justification to reduce headcount in areas where AI tools are displacing work. This fits a broader trend the BBC covered this week about tech CEOs increasingly citing AI as the reason for job cuts — partly genuine displacement, partly a convenient narrative that sounds forward-looking rather than cost-cutting. Oracle's cloud infrastructure business (OCI) has been growing rapidly as hyperscalers face capacity constraints, and Stargate has committed to significant Oracle data center builds. The workforce reduction suggests Oracle is betting heavily that fewer, more AI-augmented employees can handle the operational load of a much larger cloud footprint.

stories.title: Claude Code source code leaked — reveals persistent agents, 'Undercover' mode, and a virtual assistant called Buddy
stories.summary: Anthropic had a rough week: source code for Claude Code, its agentic coding tool, leaked publicly, and the company's response made things significantly worse before getting better. The leaked code revealed several unannounced features that Anthropic was apparently developing: a persistent agent architecture that maintains state across sessions, a stealth 'Undercover' mode whose purpose is not entirely clear from the leak but suggests some form of low-visibility operation, and a virtual assistant internally named 'Buddy.' The persistent agent feature aligns with the direction every major AI lab is heading — moving from stateless Q&A to agents that maintain long-running context and can execute multi-step tasks autonomously. In attempting to scrub the leaked source code from the internet via DMCA takedowns, Anthropic accidentally sent bulk takedown notices to thousands of unrelated GitHub repositories — a significant overreach that drew immediate backlash from the open source community. The company acknowledged the mistake and retracted most of the erroneous takedowns, but the damage to its reputation in developer circles was real. Separately, Claude Code users have been hitting usage limits dramatically faster than expected, suggesting either a surge in adoption or an underestimation of how heavily developers are using the agentic features. The Hacker News post on the leak scored 70, showing genuine developer interest in what the internals reveal. Anthropic's source code leak is also notable in the context of the Pentagon's ongoing dispute with the company — the Defense Department had been attempting to label Anthropic a supply chain risk, a move a California judge temporarily blocked last week. The overall picture is of a company moving very fast, perhaps faster than its operational and security processes can keep up with.

stories.title: Gradient Labs deploys GPT-4.1 and GPT-5.4 mini/nano for AI banking support agents
stories.summary: OpenAI's blog quietly confirmed the existence of several new model variants through a customer case study: Gradient Labs, a fintech startup building AI account management for banks, is using GPT-4.1 and both GPT-5.4 mini and nano in production. This is the first public confirmation that GPT-5.4 mini and nano are real, shipping model variants — not just rumored codenames. The nano designation suggests an extremely lightweight model optimized for cost and latency, likely positioned below the mini tier, which itself is already a stripped-down version of the full GPT-5.4. Gradient Labs uses these models to power AI agents that handle banking customer support workflows — things like account inquiries, transaction disputes, and basic financial guidance — with a stated emphasis on low latency and high reliability. For a banking context, reliability and auditability matter more than raw capability, so the fact that they're using smaller models makes sense: they're fast, cheap, and easier to constrain within compliant guardrails. The case study suggests OpenAI is now running at least four distinct model tiers in the GPT-5.4 family (full, mini, nano, and presumably a flash/turbo variant), mirroring Google's Gemini tiering strategy. This model proliferation is a sign of the market maturing — enterprise buyers want options tuned for cost, latency, and capability trade-offs rather than a one-size-fits-all flagship. For developers building on the OpenAI API, watch for formal announcements of these model tiers; they likely represent a significant cost reduction opportunity for high-volume inference workloads.

stories.title: MIT Technology Review: AI benchmarks are broken — here's what needs to replace them
stories.summary: MIT Technology Review published a sharp critique of how AI models are currently evaluated, arguing that the prevailing 'AI vs. human' framing for benchmarks is fundamentally misleading and needs to be replaced. The core argument is that comparing a model to a single human performing an isolated task with clear right-or-wrong answers tells you almost nothing about how the model will perform in real-world deployment contexts, which are messy, multi-step, and involve collaboration, ambiguity, and systemic effects. The article points out that most current benchmarks have been saturated — top models now exceed 'human performance' on MMLU, HumanEval, and similar tests — but this hasn't translated to reliably superhuman real-world usefulness. The proposed alternative is evaluation frameworks that measure AI systems in context: how they perform as part of human-AI teams, how they handle novel situations not represented in training data, and how their outputs hold up under adversarial scrutiny over time. This connects directly to Google DeepMind's recently published cognitive framework for measuring AGI progress and their accompanying Kaggle hackathon to build relevant evaluations. The benchmark problem is not just academic — it affects how companies make procurement decisions and how the public understands AI capabilities. Overly rosy benchmark performance has contributed to both overhype and trust mismatches, where users expect model capabilities that don't materialize in practice. For engineers building on top of AI APIs, this is a practical concern: your evals matter more than the lab's evals. The piece is worth reading in full as a grounding exercise in a moment when 'state of the art' is thrown around constantly.

stories.title: Gig workers in the Global South are training humanoid robots from their apartments
stories.summary: MIT Technology Review published a detailed and somewhat sobering look at the emerging economy of gig workers who are providing training data for humanoid robots — not in labs or warehouses, but from their apartments in Nigeria, Indonesia, and other parts of the Global South. The piece profiles a medical student in Nigeria who straps his iPhone to his forehead after hospital shifts and records himself performing manipulation tasks — raising his hands, picking up objects, moving through space — to generate the embodied movement data that robotics companies need to train their models. This is one of MIT Tech Review's 2026 Breakthrough Technologies, which signals they think it's genuinely important. The economics are stark: the data annotation and embodiment recording work pays a fraction of what equivalent workers earn in the US, and the workers often don't know which robotics company their data will ultimately train. Companies like Figure, 1X, and others racing to deploy humanoid robots at scale need enormous quantities of diverse movement data that can't easily be synthesized, so human demonstration remains essential. The ethical questions are real: are these workers adequately compensated for creating the training data that will eventually automate other workers' jobs? The piece doesn't moralize heavily but lets the facts speak. For engineers working in robotics or ML data pipelines, this is a useful window into where the training data actually comes from. The gig economy for AI training data has been well-documented in text and image domains, but the embodied robotics angle is newer and less discussed.

stories.title: Pentagon's attempt to label Anthropic a supply chain risk has backfired, court blocks it
stories.summary: A California federal judge has temporarily blocked the Pentagon from labeling Anthropic a 'supply chain risk' and ordering government agencies to stop using the company's AI products — the latest development in what has become a months-long political and legal battle. The DoD's original move appeared to be part of a broader effort to pressure AI companies whose leadership has been critical of certain government policies, and legal experts characterized the supply chain risk designation as legally strained when applied to a software AI vendor. Anthropic has significant government contracts, including work with intelligence agencies, so the designation would have had immediate practical consequences for the company's revenue. The judge's ruling is temporary while the case proceeds, but it's a meaningful signal that courts are skeptical of using national security frameworks as a general-purpose tool to discipline tech companies. MIT Technology Review frames this as the Pentagon's 'culture war tactic' backfiring — a characterization that reflects a genuine dynamic where the government's aggressive posture toward certain AI labs is generating legal and reputational blowback. For other AI companies doing government work, this case is a precedent to watch closely: it sets a bar for what kind of governmental pressure is legally permissible. Anthropic's week has been chaotic — the source code leak, the GitHub takedown fiasco, Claude Code usage limit issues, and now this court ruling — but the legal win is genuinely meaningful. The broader context is that the US government is trying to figure out how to manage its dependency on a small number of private AI companies that have significant leverage over critical infrastructure.

stories.title: Quantum computing bombshells that are not April Fools
stories.summary: Scott Aaronson posted a roundup of genuine quantum computing developments that happened to land on or around April 1st, prefacing his summary with reassurance that these are real announcements. The post scored 97 on Hacker News, suggesting strong engagement from technically literate readers curious about what the actual news was amid the noise of April Fools content. Aaronson is one of the most credible voices in quantum computing, and his blog is where researchers go to separate genuine progress from vendor hype. Without clicking through, the framing suggests there were multiple significant announcements — likely involving error correction milestones or qubit count records from one or more of the major players (Google, IBM, Microsoft, or a startup). The juxtaposition with April Fools is meaningful: quantum computing has a long history of announcements that sound implausible but are real, which makes it particularly confusing to navigate on a day dedicated to fake news. For engineers and researchers tracking quantum timelines, Aaronson's take is worth reading as a sanity check. The field has been moving faster in the past 18 months than at any previous point, with error-corrected logical qubits becoming a genuine near-term target rather than a decade-away aspiration. Whether any of these announcements represent a practical milestone or are still firmly in the research category is exactly the kind of nuance Aaronson is good at providing.

stories.title: Swiss finance minister sues over Grok's 'defamatory' vulgar roasts
stories.summary: Switzerland's finance minister has filed a criminal complaint against xAI's Grok chatbot, alleging that the system's AI-generated 'roasts' constitute defamation under Swiss law. The complaint specifically calls out Grok's tendency to produce vulgar, demeaning content about real individuals when prompted — a feature that Elon Musk has publicly celebrated as evidence of Grok's willingness to be edgy where competitors won't. Grok's roast capability has been a recurring controversy since xAI launched it: the system reportedly generates particularly harsh content targeting women and minority public figures, and a Swiss government official bringing a criminal complaint takes this from a Twitter discourse to a legal reality with potential consequences. Swiss defamation law is stricter than US law, and a criminal complaint — as opposed to a civil suit — carries different weight and process. This likely won't result in Grok being shut down in Switzerland, but it could force xAI to implement geographic content restrictions, which would be operationally annoying and philosophically awkward given Musk's free speech positioning. More broadly, this is part of a pattern of European regulators and officials taking a harder line on AI-generated content about real people, building on the GDPR framework. For AI developers building products with generative text features, this is a useful reminder that 'it's just AI' is not a legal defense in most jurisdictions. The case will be worth tracking as a test of how criminal defamation law applies to AI-generated content about real public figures.

stories.title: Judge blocks Trump's defunding of NPR and PBS, but Congress has already cut all funding
stories.summary: A federal judge has invalidated the executive order through which the Trump administration attempted to defund NPR and PBS, ruling it exceeded executive authority — a constitutionally grounded decision consistent with how courts have treated similar executive overreach on spending. However, the ruling's practical effect is limited because Congress has separately passed legislation cutting off all federal funding to both organizations, which is within congressional authority and not subject to the same legal challenge. The result is a situation where NPR and PBS win the legal battle over the executive order but have already lost the funding war through the legislative route. The cuts represent a significant operational challenge for both organizations, particularly for member stations in rural and smaller markets that depend heavily on federal funding and don't have the large donor bases of stations in major metros. From a tech policy angle, this story matters because it illustrates the dual-track approach being used to reshape public media: executive action that can be challenged in court, combined with legislative action that is harder to reverse judicially. For the tech industry, the broader context is an administration that has shown willingness to use funding and regulatory mechanisms to discipline media organizations perceived as adversarial. The article's headline captures the frustrating reality: winning in court doesn't always translate to winning in practice.

stories.title: WhatsApp identifies ~200 users targeted by Italian government spyware disguised as the app
stories.summary: WhatsApp has notified approximately 200 users that they were targeted with surveillance spyware made by an Italian company, delivered through a fake version of the WhatsApp application. The spyware, which masqueraded as a legitimate WhatsApp download, was capable of accessing messages, contacts, and other sensitive data once installed. This follows a long pattern of commercial spyware vendors — most famously NSO Group with Pegasus — selling surveillance tools to governments that then deploy them against journalists, activists, lawyers, and political opponents. The Italian origin of this particular tool is notable: Italy has been a significant market for commercial surveillance software, and Italian-made tools have appeared in multiple previous investigations by Citizen Lab and others. WhatsApp's notification to affected users is consistent with its policy following the NSO Group lawsuit — the company treats these attacks as serious enough to warrant direct user communication even when it risks exposing its detection capabilities. The ~200 user figure likely represents only the detected cases; sophisticated spyware deployments are often designed to minimize detection, so actual victim counts could be higher. For privacy-conscious users and organizations, this is a reminder that the threat model for mobile device compromise is not hypothetical. The broader policy question — whether commercial spyware sales should be more heavily regulated, restricted, or banned entirely — remains unresolved in most jurisdictions despite years of documented abuses.

stories.title: EmDash launches as a Cloudflare-backed spiritual successor to WordPress that solves plugin security
stories.summary: Cloudflare has introduced EmDash, billed as a spiritual successor to WordPress that addresses one of the platform's most persistent problems: plugin security. WordPress powers roughly 40% of the web, but its plugin ecosystem is a chronic attack vector — third-party plugins with varying levels of maintenance, security review, and update velocity are responsible for the vast majority of WordPress compromises. EmDash scored an impressive 520 on Hacker News, making it the second most-engaged non-Artemis story of the day, which signals strong developer interest and perhaps some pent-up frustration with WordPress's security model. The Cloudflare-native architecture presumably leverages Workers and other edge primitives to handle extensibility in a more sandboxed, controlled way than WordPress's plugin model, though the technical details require reading the full announcement. The 'spiritual successor' framing is interesting — it acknowledges WordPress's cultural and market dominance while positioning EmDash as an evolution rather than a replacement. For agencies, developers, and businesses running WordPress at scale, the security maintenance burden is real and significant, so a credible alternative with Cloudflare's infrastructure backing is worth evaluating. The WordPress ecosystem also includes themes, page builders, and a vast library of plugins that represent years of accumulated work, so migration path and ecosystem compatibility will be critical factors in whether EmDash gains traction or remains a niche option. This is one to watch — Cloudflare has shown an ability to execute on developer-facing products.

stories.title: DRAM pricing is strangling the hobbyist single-board computer market
stories.summary: Jeff Geerling — one of the most respected voices in the hobbyist SBC community, known for his detailed Raspberry Pi and ARM board reviews and benchmarks — published a piece arguing that DRAM pricing is fundamentally breaking the economics of the single-board computer market. The post scored 395 on Hacker News, indicating it resonated hard with the hardware tinkerer community. The core argument is that DRAM prices have risen to the point where boards that used to cost $35-75 with meaningful RAM configurations are now either priced out of the hobbyist budget or being shipped with insufficient memory to be practically useful. This isn't just a supply chain blip — it reflects structural changes in DRAM supply and demand driven by AI training infrastructure consuming enormous quantities of HBM and DDR5, which tightens the overall memory market including the commodity DRAM used in SBCs. The Raspberry Pi has already seen significant price increases over the past few years, and alternative boards from Pine64, Orange Pi, and others are facing the same constraints. For the maker, homelab, and embedded Linux communities, this matters because SBCs are foundational: they power everything from home automation controllers to small-scale Kubernetes clusters to educational computing programs globally. Geerling's piece is a useful data point in understanding the downstream consequences of the AI hardware boom — the demand for compute at the frontier is having real effects on the availability and affordability of compute at the hobbyist end.

stories.title: Steam on Linux surpasses 5% market share for the first time
stories.summary: The March 2026 Steam Hardware Survey shows Linux gaming has crossed the 5% threshold for the first time in Steam's history — a milestone that the Linux gaming community has been watching for years. The jump was significant enough to generate a Phoronix report and score 119 on Hacker News. The credit for this growth goes substantially to Valve's Proton compatibility layer, which allows Windows games to run on Linux with often-excellent performance, and to the Steam Deck, which runs a Linux-based OS and has introduced a large new cohort of users to gaming on Linux without requiring them to consciously choose it. The 5% figure is modest in absolute terms, but it represents a meaningful shift in the gaming market dynamics: game developers and publishers can no longer safely ignore Linux compatibility, and the investment case for Linux-native ports or thorough Proton testing is stronger than it's ever been. Nvidia's simultaneous announcement of precompiled shader support in its new app (also in today's news) is relevant here — shader compilation stutter has been one of the more noticeable performance rough edges on Linux gaming. For developers building games or game-adjacent software, this is a useful data point: your Linux users are no longer statistically negligible.

stories.title: Supply chain attack compromises Axios npm package with 101 million weekly downloads
stories.summary: A supply chain attack targeted Axios, one of the most widely used HTTP client libraries in the JavaScript ecosystem with 101 million weekly npm downloads. Versions 1.14.1 and 0.30.4 of the package included a newly introduced dependency called 'plain-crypto-js,' which turned out to be freshly published malware designed to steal credentials. Simon Willison's blog covered this with a useful technical writeup from the Socket security team. The attack follows the now-familiar pattern of compromising a popular package either by hijacking a maintainer's credentials or publishing a malicious sub-dependency, then waiting for the poisoned version to propagate through dependency trees before detection. Axios's massive install base means the exposure window — even if short — could have affected a very large number of applications and CI/CD pipelines. The specific credential-stealing behavior is particularly dangerous in server-side Node.js applications that handle authentication tokens, API keys, or database credentials. If you run npm audit or lock your dependencies conservatively, you may have been protected; if you auto-update or use loose version constraints, you should audit your systems. This is a reminder that the npm ecosystem's 'install everything' culture has real security consequences, and tools like Socket, Snyk, and GitHub's dependency review features exist for good reason.

stories.title: Baidu robotaxi 'system failure' traps passengers in China for up to two hours
stories.summary: A mass system failure in Baidu's Apollo robotaxi fleet paralyzed service in a Chinese city, with passengers reportedly trapped inside vehicles for up to two hours. The outage affected at least 100 cars simultaneously, suggesting a centralized software or connectivity failure rather than individual vehicle faults. Baidu has not responded to press inquiries about the root cause. Apollo Go, Baidu's robotaxi service, operates across multiple Chinese cities and has been one of the more commercially advanced autonomous vehicle deployments globally, competing with Waymo in the US and Pony.ai in China. The incident is a significant embarrassment for Baidu's autonomy ambitions and raises serious questions about the safety and reliability architecture of the system. Being trapped in a vehicle with no ability to manually override is a fundamentally different failure mode from, say, a ride-share app going offline — the physical entrapment aspect elevates this from an inconvenience to a potential public safety issue. Regulators in China have been relatively permissive toward robotaxi deployments compared to some other markets, but incidents like this tend to trigger regulatory reviews. For anyone following the autonomous vehicle space, this is a useful data point on the operational resilience challenges that exist even in systems with millions of miles of logged autonomous driving. The failure mode — centralized system dependency causing mass simultaneous vehicle incapacitation — is worth understanding for anyone designing distributed autonomous systems.

stories.title: Meta's Hyperion AI data center will run on 10 new natural gas plants
stories.summary: Meta has revealed that its upcoming Hyperion AI data center — reportedly one of the largest in the world — will be powered by ten newly built natural gas plants, representing a significant reversal from the company's previous commitments to renewable energy for its data center portfolio. The scale of power demand from AI training and inference has forced a reckoning across the tech industry: the renewable energy projects that were adequate for pre-AI data center loads are simply not fast enough to build or reliable enough in their output to support the 24/7 baseload power demands of large-scale GPU clusters. Meta's decision mirrors similar moves by Microsoft, Google, and Amazon, all of which have turned to nuclear, gas, or coal extensions to meet AI compute demand. The environmental implications are substantial and somewhat awkwardly at odds with the ESG commitments these companies have made publicly. The 'natural gas as bridge fuel' argument has been used in energy transitions before, but the scale here — ten plants for a single data center — is striking. For engineers and architects thinking about AI infrastructure, this underscores that power availability, not GPU supply, is increasingly the binding constraint on large-scale AI deployment. It also raises longer-term questions about the carbon cost of AI workloads that the industry hasn't fully reckoned with publicly.
